1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Hybrid applications have become increasingly popular. Instead of rewriting an existing web application, many teams embed their web dashboards or reporting systems directly inside a Flutter application using a WebView.
Flutter makes this easy through packages like webview_flutter and flutter_inappwebview. While both are excellent choices, flutter_inappwebview provides more advanced features such as JavaScript handlers, custom script injection, download interception, and deeper control over the browser lifecycle.
In this article, we’ll learn how to inject JavaScript into a Flutter WebView so that Flutter can listen to events happening inside the webpage.
Our goal is simple:

• Detect downloads created entirely in JavaScript

• Intercept Blob URLs and Data URLs

• Send the generated file back to Flutter

• Save the file natively on the mobile device
By the end of this guide, your Flutter application won’t just display a webpage — it will actively communicate with it.
The Problem
Many modern web applications no longer download files using a normal HTTP request.
Instead, JavaScript generates files dynamically:

• Excel reports

• PDF exports

• CSV files

• PNG screenshots

• Canvas-generated images
A typical implementation looks like this:
const blob = new Blob([data]);
const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);const link = document.createElement("a");
link.href = url;
link.download = "report.xlsx";
link.click();Or a screenshot might be downloaded using:
canvas.toDataURL("image/png");The problem is that these files only exist inside the browser’s memory.
Because there is no actual network request, Flutter’s normal download callbacks are never triggered. As a result, the mobile application has no idea that a file has just been generated.
The Solution: JavaScript Injection
Instead of waiting for Flutter to detect a download, we inject our own JavaScript into the webpage before it loads.
The injected script acts like a bridge between JavaScript and Flutter.
It can:

• Track every Blob URL created by the webpage.

• Listen for download events.

• Intercept programmatically triggered downloads.

• Convert Blob or Data URLs into Base64.

• Send the file directly to Flutter through a JavaScript handler.
The communication flow becomes:
Web Page
│
▼
JavaScript creates Blob/Data URL
│
▼
Injected Blob Interceptor
│
▼
Flutter JavaScript Handler
│
▼
Decode Base64
│
▼
Save File NativelyStep 1 — Add flutter_inappwebview
First, add the package to your pubspec.yaml.
dependencies:
flutter_inappwebview: ^5.4.3+7Step 2 — Create a WebView Controller
We’ll keep a reference to the WebView so we can communicate with it later.
InAppWebViewController? _controller;final Completer<InAppWebViewController> _controllerCompleter =
Completer<InAppWebViewController>();Step 3 — Inject Our JavaScript
Now comes the interesting part.
We’ll create a JavaScript interceptor that keeps track of every Blob URL created by the webpage, intercepts download events, and forwards the generated file to Flutter.
(Insert your _blobInterceptorScript here.)
Step 4 — Inject the Script Before the Page Loads
To make sure every webpage event can be intercepted, inject the script at AT_DOCUMENT_START.
initialUserScripts: UnmodifiableListView<UserScript>([
UserScript(
source: _blobInterceptorScript,
injectionTime: UserScriptInjectionTime.AT_DOCUMENT_START,
),
]),Injecting at this stage ensures our interceptor is available before any webpage JavaScript executes.
Step 5 — Receive Events in Flutter
Whenever the injected JavaScript captures a Blob or Data URL download, it sends the file to Flutter using callHandler().
On the Flutter side, register a JavaScript handler to receive the data.
(Insert your addJavaScriptHandler() implementation here.)
The received Base64 string is decoded into bytes and can then be saved using a MethodChannel or any native storage implementation.
Final Thoughts
JavaScript injection transforms a WebView from a passive browser into a two-way communication bridge between your web application and Flutter.
Although this article focused on intercepting Blob and Data URL downloads, the same technique can be used to listen for almost any webpage event, including custom JavaScript events, DOM interactions, authentication callbacks, analytics events, payment results, or communication with existing web applications.
Once you understand JavaScript injection, you’ll realize that a WebView doesn’t have to be an isolated browser — it can become a seamless extension of your Flutter application.
